value.description:
Read more facts and information on the Westie page.

The West Highland White Terrier, or Westie, originated in Scotland in the 19th century, developed to hunt small game like rats and foxes. They are known for their confident, lively, and friendly temperament. Westies make excellent family pets and are great with children, though their strong prey drive may make them chase small animals. An interesting fact is that Westies were bred to be white to avoid being mistaken for prey during hunts. Breed standard coat color is pure white; no other colors are permitted. Their number one health concern is skin allergies, though they are also prone to patellar luxation (knee issues) and craniomandibular osteopathy (jaw bone disorder). Regular grooming is needed to maintain their double coat.

value.description:
Read more facts and information on the Whippet page.

The Whippet originated in England in the 19th century, developed by working-class people for racing and hunting small game like rabbits. They are known for their gentle, affectionate, and calm temperament. Whippets make excellent family pets and are great with children, though their thin build makes them sensitive to rough play. Whippets are one of the fastest dog breeds, capable of reaching speeds over 38 mph. Breed standard coat colors include a wide variety, such as black, white, brindle, fawn, and blue; no colors are specifically prohibited. Their number one health concern is heart issues, particularly mitral valve disease, though they are also prone to skin injuries due to their thin coats. Regular exercise and a warm environment are important for their well-being.

value.description:
Read more facts and information on the Xolo page.

The Xoloitzcuintli, or Xolo, is an ancient breed from Mexico, dating back over 3,000 years. They were traditionally used as companions, guardians, and even for healing rituals by the Aztecs. Xolos are known for their calm, loyal, and alert temperament. They make excellent family pets and are good with children, though they can be reserved with strangers. The breed is relatively rare outside of Mexico but is gaining popularity. An interesting fact is that Xolos come in three sizes (toy, miniature, and standard) and two varieties: hairless and coated. Breed standard coat colors for hairless Xolos include dark shades like black, gray, and bronze, while coated Xolos can have any color; no specific colors are prohibited. Their number one health concern is dental issues, particularly in hairless varieties due to missing teeth. Skin care is also important for hairless Xolos to prevent sunburn and dryness.

value.description:
Read more facts and information on the Yorkie page.

The Yorkshire Terrier, or Yorkie, originated in England in the 19th century, developed by working-class weavers to catch rats in textile mills. They are known for their bold, energetic, and affectionate temperament. Yorkies make excellent family pets and are great for small living spaces, though their small size makes them better suited for homes with older children. An interesting fact is that Yorkies were once much larger but were selectively bred to become the small, portable companions we know today. Breed standard coat colors are blue and tan; no solid colors or other combinations are permitted. Their number one health concern is patellar luxation (knee issues), though they are also prone to dental problems due to their small mouths. Regular grooming is essential to maintain their long, silky coat.

value.description: The Yugoslavian Shepherd Dog, also known as the Sharplanina, is a large sized dog breed that originated in Yugoslavia. It was developed as a herding dog, used for guarding and herding livestock in the harsh, mountainous regions of Yugoslavia. The breed is known for its strong protective instincts, high energy, and loyalty, making it a popular choice for families and farmers alike. The standard coat colors for the Yugoslavian Shepherd Dog range from white to dark brown-spotting and markings are not permitted. Despite its popularity in its native country, the breed is relatively rare outside of Yugoslavia. The Yugoslavian Shepherd Dog is a protective breed that requires a lot of exercise and mental stimulation, making it best suited for experienced dog owners who have the time and energy to provide for its needs. With its strong protective instincts and loyal nature, the Yugoslavian Shepherd Dog is a well-regarded breed that is sure to bring security and peace of mind to its owners.
